To calculate the probability that the average interaction time for a random sample of 30 users in Version B is less than the average interaction time for a random sample of 30 users in Version A, we can use the properties of the sampling distribution of the difference between two means. Since the sample sizes are large enough to apply the Central Limit Theorem, the distribution of the difference in sample means will be approximately normal. The mean of the difference in sample means is the difference in population means, which is $ 45 - 40 = 5 $ seconds. The standard deviation of the difference in sample means is calculated using the formula $ \sqrt{\frac{\sigma_A^2}{n_A} + \frac{\sigma_B^2}{n_B}} $, where $ \sigma_A = 5 $, $ \sigma_B = 3 $, and $ n_A = n_B = 30 $. This gives $ \sqrt{\frac{25}{30} + \frac{9}{30}} = \sqrt{\frac{34}{30}} \approx 1.06 $. The z-score for the difference in means is $ \frac{0 - 5}{1.06} \approx -4.72 $. Using the standard normal distribution, the probability that the average interaction time for Version B is less than that for Version A is the cumulative probability up to this z-score, which is approximately 0.000009 or 0.0009%. This indicates that it is extremely unlikely for the average interaction time of Version B to be less than that of Version A.
